title: Shattered Horizon
blurb: One engineer built the future. Another burned it to the ground. Now she watches from the shadows as her life’s work collapses—one rusted bolt at a time. Vengeance or exposure? She has 47 days to choose.
comparable: For fans of *The Lies of Locke Lamora* by Scott Lynch, *Project Hail Mary* by Andy Weir, and *The Traitor Baru Cormorant* by Seth Dickinson

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The Deep Cut hummed at 72% capacity today—warm air laced with ozone, metal groaning under the strain of another standard rotation. Linnea Voss adjusted her wrist display, fingers tapping against the alloy railing as the structural integrity scan rolled across the habitation ring’s ribs. The numbers stayed green. For now.

Grit leaned against the access ladder, arms crossed, his face half-hidden in the emergency strobe’s flicker. “You’re squinting at the wrong elevation,” he said. “Strut twenty-three’s playin’ tricks again.”

She didn’t look up. “Strut twenty-three’s just fine.”

“Then why’d you just adjust the calipers on your terminal?”

Linnea exhaled through her nose, kept her voice level. “Because the calipers were off by six millimeters. And if you’d pulled your head out of the maintenance logs long enough to notice, you’d see the scan’s already corrected it.”

Grit pushed off the ladder, boots clanging on the grating. He didn’t argue—just stepped closer, close enough for the scent of recycled grease and burnt copper to cut through the station’s antiseptic air. His fingers hovered over the rail, then tapped where the strut met the deck plate. A hairline shift. Barely there.

“You fixed it before the scan finished,” he said. “Again.”

Linnea adjusted the wrist display to show the before-and-after overlay. Third rev, strut twenty-three had been misaligned by point six degrees. Fourth rev, it was perfect. No one else would ever see the difference.

“Efficiency,” she said. “Same as always.”

Grit wiped a smear of lubricant off the rail with his thumb, flicked it onto the deck. “Bullshit efficiency. You’re bleeding time. And time’s what we don’t got when the OBA starts screaming about quota.”

Linnea clipped the display back onto her belt. “Then maybe you should’ve fixed it before I got here.”

A beat. The station creaked around them—hydraulics sighing, pumps thrumming, the distant clatter of a maintenance bot dragging a pallet of stabilizer foam. Grit’s gaze dropped to the cracked data slate in her hand, the one she’d used to log the correction. He knew what it was. They both did.

“You’re logging anomalies,” he said. “Not fixes.”

“Semantics.”

“Semantics get people killed when the directors start looking for scapegoats.”

Linnea pocketed the slate. “They won’t find anything.”

Grit exhaled, short and sharp. “You keep believin’ that.”

The habitation ring shuddered. Not a quake—just the rhythmic pulse of the station settling, the sound of a thousand tons of steel breathing. Linnea’s fingers twitched. She counted the seconds between pulses. Thirty-two. Normal. She relaxed.

Grit’s wrist display chirped. He tapped it, frowned. “They’re rerouting power to Bay 7. Some kind of emergency draw.”

Linnea didn’t move. “Not my deck.”

“No. But it’s coming from the secondary grid.”

She finally looked at him. “You saying it’s my fault?”

“I’m saying it’s your grid. And your grid’s been behave—”

A loudspeaker crackled overhead. Distorted at first, then clear.

“Attention, Corio Station. This is Director Rourke. All department heads report to The Vault for an immediate structural integrity briefing. That is all.”

The line went dead.

Grit’s jaw tightened. “Forty-seven minutes. That’s all the heads-up we get.”

Linnea’s pulse didn’t spike. It didn’t slow. It just… adjusted. Like the calibration on a thruster nozzle under load. “Then we’d better move.”

She took the first step toward the ladder, then stopped when Grit didn’t follow.

“Linnea.”

She turned. He held out his hand.

Not for a handshake. For the data slate.

She held it behind her back. “I’ve got it.”

“You log it here, you log it everywhere. Rourke’s got eyes on the terminal feeds.”

“Then I’ll log it later.”

“Or you let me take it. I’ll get it to the Vault. You walk in clean.”

She looked at his outstretched hand. The callouses. The old burn scar across his knuckles. Seven years. They’d come up together. He knew parts of the truth. Not all of it.

Linnea slipped the slate into her pocket. “I’ll log it myself. In person.”

Grit’s gaze flicked to the pocket, then back to her face. He didn’t argue. Didn’t push.

“Suit yourself,” he said. “But remember—paper trail’s the only thing keeping you alive in there.”

Linnea walked to the ladder. Placed a boot on the first rung.

Grit’s hand clamped down on her shoulder—not hard, not gentle. Stopped her.

She turned.

He held up a cracked circuit board, the one she’d used to run the diagnostic. Not the data slate. The slate was hidden. This was different.

“You dropped this,” he said.

She took it.

It was warm. Fresh from her pocket.

Grit stepped back. “Better clean your act up, boss. Or you’re gonna get us all killed.”

Linnea pocketed the circuit board. Climbed the ladder. The rungs were cold under her gloves.

At the top, she glanced back. Grit watched her go, his face half in shadow, half in the glow of a failing overhead panel.

She didn’t smile. Didn’t nod.

She just walked away.

Ahead, the corridor stretched into the dim, the air thick with the scent of recirculated sweat and industrial cleaner. The Deep Cut was just another rung on the ladder to nowhere. But Corio Station—her station—was still standing.

For now.

She turned the corner. The emergency lights flickered once, twice.

When the glow steadied, Grit was gone.

Chapter 2

The Vault smelled of ozone and old paper. Linnea Voss stepped through the security frame, her boots clicking on the polished deck plates. The room hummed—fans spinning at low speed, servers thrumming—while fifty directors and engineers already sat in curved rows facing the curved wall of screens.

Silvano Rourke stood at the podium, his suit jacket draped over the back of his chair like a claim staked. He didn’t look up when Linnea entered. She took her usual seat in the third row, hands folded on the console in front of her. The display glowed blue around her wrists, logging her arrival.

Grit materialized beside her. He didn’t sit. Just leaned against the seatback, arms crossed, eyes on the podium.

“You’re late,” he murmured.

“By twelve seconds. They’ll survive.”

“Rourke won’t.”

The lights dimmed. Rourke tapped the podium once, twice—the mic caught the taps, broadcast them like gunshots. The room hushed.

“Good afternoon, Corio leadership,” Rourke said. His voice carried authority, not volume. “We’re here to discuss structural vulnerabilities identified in this morning’s audit.”

Linnea’s fingers curled slightly. The console screen shifted, displaying a schematic of Corio Station’s spine—the central load-bearing truss. Red nodes pulsed at three locations.

Rourke let the image hang for three seconds.

“These anomalies,” he said, “were logged by Chief Structural Integrity Engineer Linnea Voss. Corrected by Director Elias Marr. And then—” He paused. Smiled. It didn’t reach his eyes. “—covered up.”

A beat of silence. Then whispers rippled through the room.

Linnea’s jaw tightened. She didn’t look at Grit.

Rourke tapped the podium again. The schematic expanded—green correction vectors overlaid in real time.

“According to revised logs,” he said, “the original scan identified a stress fracture in Strut 23-Delta. Primary engineer recorded the anomaly, applied a fix—without authorization—and then scrubbed the error from the terminal feed.”

The screens shifted. A timestamped log glowed on the wall: *Voss, L. – Anomaly noted. Correction applied. Recorded. Distributed.*

But beneath it, in smaller font, older: *Voss, L. – Anomaly corrected. Original scan altered. Evidence suppressed.*

Rourke tapped once more. The overlay vanished. A new image appeared—the full station blueprint, every strut, every beam. Linnea’s name flashed at the top in bold white text.

Then it vanished too.

In its place: *Revised Design Protocol – Outer Belt Authority Engineering Division.*

Linnea’s breath stayed even. She stared straight ahead. The other directors shifted in their seats. Some looked at her. Some looked at Rourke. Some avoided both.

Rourke cleared his throat. “Chief Engineer Voss has demonstrated a pattern of unauthorized modifications to critical systems. These actions compromise safety, violate protocol, and undermine the Outer Belt Authority’s operational integrity.”

He paused. Let the silence press down.

Linnea’s spine didn’t bend. Her pulse didn’t race. It calibrated. Like the station’s stabilizers tuning themselves under load.

“Consequently,” Rourke continued, “I am reassigning oversight of structural integrity to Director Elias Marr. Effective immediately.”

Grit didn’t move. Didn’t react. His jaw might have been carved from steel.

Rourke stepped back from the podium. The screens flipped to a live feed of Corio’s outer truss. Strut 23-Delta pulsed red—real time, real data.

“Director Marr will lead tomorrow’s emergency drill,” Rourke said. “One hour. All department heads required. That includes you, Captain Duvall.”

Jace Duvall stood in the back row. Linnea hadn’t noticed him enter. He met her gaze for a fraction of a second—long enough to register something unreadable—then looked down at his display.

Rourke dismissed the room with a nod. The lights brightened. Chairs scraped. Voices rose in muted conversation. No one approached Linnea.

Grit finally exhaled. Short. Controlled. “You knew it was coming.”

She didn’t answer.

He leaned in closer, voice low. “You should’ve logged it. Let it go through channels.”

“Then it would’ve been public.”

“Now it is public. Just not the way you wanted.”

Linnea tapped a command into her wrist display. A data slate pinged on the network. She watched the signal propagate—through The Vault, down the spine, into The Deep Cut. It reached a maintenance panel. Stopped.

Grit’s eyes flicked to her wrist. To the slate icon. To the panel half a klick below.

“You’re broadcasting,” he said.

She killed the transmission.

Rourke stepped off the podium, moving toward the exit with a delegation of directors. He passed Jace Duvall. The two men exchanged a few words, too quiet for Linnea to catch. Jace nodded once. Rourke clapped him on the shoulder.

Linnea stood, smoothed her uniform. The hum of the room settled into white noise. She turned toward the exit.

Grit caught her elbow. “Where are you going?”

“To log the anomaly.”

“You just watched Rourke erase your name. You think a new log’ll fix that?”

“I’m not logging for the record.”

She pulled free and walked out. The Vault’s airlock hissed shut behind her. The corridor beyond was narrower, dimmer. Emergency lighting cast long shadows.

Jace Duvall leaned against the wall twenty meters away. Arms crossed. Waiting.

Linnea didn’t slow.

He pushed off the wall as she approached. “Chief Voss.”

She kept walking. “Captain.”

He fell into step beside her. “You’re not staying for the drill prep.”

“Not my station anymore.”

“According to Rourke.”

She stopped. Turned. Looked at him—the sharp jawline, the quiet intensity, the way his security insignia caught the low light.

“I didn’t design this station for amateurs,” she said.

Jace didn’t flinch. “You designed it for someone who knew what she was doing.”

She held his gaze. Three seconds. Four. His pupils didn’t dilate. His breath didn’t hitch.

Then he looked away. Not down. Not sideways. Just… away.

“You’re still running the numbers,” he said. “Aren’t you?”

Linnea said nothing.

Jace nodded once, like he’d already decided the answer. “The drill tomorrow. Don’t show up.”

She watched him walk back toward The Vault. His boots didn’t echo. The station did.

She turned the opposite way—toward the lower decks. Toward The Deep Cut.

And then she stopped.

She wasn’t wearing gloves. Her bare hand brushed the bulkhead. A hidden panel clicked open. Inside: a single data chip.

Not the one Grit had hidden.

A different one.

She tapped it once against her palm. Then she walked away.

No one saw her take it.

Chapter 3

Linnea’s quarters were too quiet.

She stood in the center of the module, arms loose at her sides, staring at the viewport where Corio Station spun against the void. The reflection of the overhead light made her eyes look hollow. She blinked once, twice. The image didn’t blur.

Her wrist display chimed. Another structural log. Another anomaly in Strut 47-Echo. She dismissed it without reading the details. The screen darkened.

Footsteps echoed in the corridor outside. Not urgent. Not hurried. Grit.

She tapped the door lock. It disengaged with a soft sigh. The moment it cracked open, Elias Marr stepped in, his bulk filling the threshold. He didn’t knock. Didn’t ask.

He held up a data slate. Black, cracked screen. One corner dented like it had been dropped.

“You left this in your office,” he said. “Again.”

Linnea didn’t move. “I didn’t.”

Grit tossed it onto the small fold-out table beside her protein synthesizer. It skidded, spun, then settled with a thud. The screen stayed dark.

“Then someone else did,” he said. “I found it on my terminal two days ago. Cross-referenced the signature. It’s yours. Last active file was the original load-bearing integrity model. Untagged. Unlocked.”

She exhaled through her nose. “And you kept it.”

“I kept it from Rourke.” He stepped inside, let the door close behind him. The coupling seal hissed. “You know what happens if he sees that.”

She looked at the slate. Then at him.

“He’d recognize the design,” she said.

“He’d recognize *you*,” Grit corrected. “That file has your retinal print on it. Your voiceprint. Your damn signature embedded in the encryption. If he gets ahold of it, he doesn’t just steal the station. He burns you.”

Linnea crossed to the viewport. The smear of Earth’s light still twinkled on the horizon beyond Jupiter’s shadow. A mining barge glinted as it rotated toward the refinery belt.

“So why bring it to me?” she asked.

Grit’s jaw tightened. His fingers twitched at his sides. “Because you’re going to do something stupid with it.”

She almost smiled. Almost.

“Define stupid.”

“Publishing it. Leaking it. Walking into The Vault and slapping it on Rourke’s desk with your name written in blood.”

She turned slowly. “I don’t sign in blood.”

“You might as well.”

A silence settled between them. Not comfortable. Not hostile. Just… weighted.

Grit wiped a smudge from the display with his thumb. “I kept it because I knew you’d try to use it. And I couldn’t let you.”

She stepped closer. Close enough to see the scar above his eyebrow—from the shunt installation at sixteen years old. Close enough to smell the grease and sweat clinging to his coveralls.

“You couldn’t let me?” she asked. “Or you couldn’t let yourself be the one who burned it?”

He didn’t flinch. “Both.”

She reached out. Not for the slate. For his wrist. She lifted it slightly, checking the time. Her fingers brushed the chrono display.

“You’re due in The Deep Cut in forty-three minutes,” she said. “Audit prep. Rourke’s making sure you look good in front of the directors.”

“Not my idea.”

“Doesn’t matter whose.”

She let go. He didn’t pull back. They stood there, two engineers who’d known each other longer than they’d known their own parents, the slate between them like a grenade with the pin pulled.

“Grit,” she said, “did you ever ask yourself why Rourke really wants the station running smooth?”

He exhaled sharply. “Because he’s an egomaniac.”

“Because he’s running scared.” She tapped the slate with her knuckle. “That file proves the station wasn’t built with standard load tolerances. It was optimized for efficiency, not redundancy. That’s how we stretched the resources, kept the costs down. But if the stress increases—if the belt hits a shockwave, a collision, even a damn solar flare—certain struts will fail first. Not collapse. *Fail.* Like a hinge on a rusted gate.”

Grit’s eyes narrowed. “You’re saying we’re one miscalculation from a cascade.”

“I’m saying Corio isn’t built to survive a crisis. It’s built to *operate* during one. Rourke knows that. That’s why he needs iron control—because the second the truth gets out, the investors start asking questions. The shareholders start pulling shares. The Authority starts asking who approved a design that skirts every safety standard in the Jovian code.”

Grit’s shoulders squared. “So what? You publish the file. Let it burn everything down.”

She shook her head. “No. I let it burn *him* down.”

He laughed. Not a laugh. A sharp, humorless punch of air. “Linnea, you can’t—”

“You think I haven’t thought it through?” She cut him off. “Rourke’s not just stealing my name. He’s gambling the whole colony on a design he doesn’t understand. When the stress spikes—and it will—he’ll blame the original engineer. The one who’s already been erased.”

Grit’s hands clenched into fists. “You’re talking about letting people die.”

She met his gaze. Held it. “I’m talking about making sure he dies first.”

The words hung in the air between them like ionized gas.

Grit’s breath came faster. His chest rose and fell. He looked at the slate. Then at her. Then back at the slate.

“You’re not taking it,” he said.

She didn’t answer.

He reached for it. His fingers wrapped around the cracked edge. He lifted it. Turned it over. A rusted pipe ran along the bulkhead above the small service hatch—one of a dozen used for fluid routing in The Edge.

He set the slate down.

“Do what you want with it,” he said. “I don’t care anymore.”

He turned toward the door.

Linnea didn’t move. Didn’t speak. Didn’t reach for the slate.

The door hissed open. Grit paused in the threshold. One hand braced against the frame. His voice was low, rough.

“You’re gonna get us both killed.”

She still didn’t reply.

He stepped into the corridor. The door sealed behind him with a quiet *thud.*

Linnea waited. Counted the seconds. Twenty. Thirty.

Then she turned.

Walked to the table. Looked at the slate. Didn’t pick it up.

She walked to the viewport instead. Watched Corio Station rotate. The lights of The Deep Cut flickered as a load balancer cycled. A distant clang echoed through the metal bones of the station.

She reached into her pocket. Pulled out a thin metal rod, no thicker than her thumb. A maintenance override. She tapped it against the small service panel beside the food synthesizer.

The panel clicked open. Inside: a single data chip. Sleek. Black. No labels. No serial numbers.

She tapped it once against her palm. Then she walked out.

No one saw her take it.

The door sealed behind her.

The slate stayed on the table.

Chapter 4

The observation deck at Corio Hub hummed with the low thrum of life support and the occasional chime of a console. Through the panoramic viewport, Silvano Rourke watched Earth’s pale glow hang in the black beyond Jupiter’s limb. The feed was live. In three minutes, the Outer Belt Authority directors on Luna would see him standing in his own creation.

Dr. Nia Tarkov adjusted her glasses, her fingers tapping against a data slate. The edge of the station’s central spire glinted as it rotated into view—another stunning shot, another triumph of human engineering.

Rourke kept his posture relaxed, hands clasped behind his back. “Thirty seconds to go live,” he said. “Nia, you’re up first. Tell them about the latest mineral yield projections.”

Tarkov didn’t look up. “The cobalt output is up 12% from last quarter. The automated harvester arrays are performing beyond spec. But—”

“No buts,” Rourke interrupted. “Not today.”

She finally met his eyes. The lights overhead caught the silver threading her dark hair. “Director, the power fluctuations in The Deep Cut aren’t just noise. They match a pattern.”

A muscle in Rourke’s jaw twitched. “A pattern you don’t understand.”

“I understand the grid,” she said. “These spikes aren’t random. They’re responses to load shifts. Predictable responses. Ones that match the original non-standard energy routing.”

Rourke exhaled through his nose. “You’re talking about my engineers. Your own team. They know the system.”

“They know the *current* system,” Tarkov corrected. “Not the original. Not the one that prioritized efficiency over redundancy.”

The deck’s status display pulsed amber. Five seconds. Rourke stepped forward, placed a hand on her shoulder. Not a pat. A firm, proprietary squeeze.

“Nia,” he said, voice low and smooth, “you’re brilliant. But brilliant people sometimes see ghosts in the data. We’ve been over this. The station is stable.”

She didn’t flinch under his grip. “Then let me present the findings. Full transparency builds trust with the investors.”

Rourke removed his hand. Smiled. A practiced, unreadable smile. “Transparency is important. But so is stability. The investors came here to see order. To see a station that runs like a machine. Not a science experiment.”

The countdown hit zero. The feed went live. Earth’s green and blue swirled on the main display as the transmission locked.

Rourke stepped into the frame, squared his shoulders.

“Director Silvano Rourke, Outer Belt Authority,” he began, voice crisp and confident. “Welcome to Corio Station, the jewel of the Jovian system. A testament to human ingenuity under pressure.”

On the screen, the faces of six directors flickered into view, their expressions shifting from interest to dutiful approval.

Rourke gestured toward the viewport. “As you can see, we are operating at peak efficiency. Our mineral exports are at an all-time high. Our life support systems are scrubbing air cleaner than Earth’s atmosphere. And our structural integrity? Unequaled.”

He paused, allowing the weight of his words to settle.

“Some ask how we achieved this. The answer is simple: leadership. Vision. And yes—exceptional engineering.” His gaze flicked toward Tarkov for the briefest moment. “A team that understands the difference between building a structure and building a legacy.”

Tarkov’s fingers stilled on the slate. Her jaw tightened.

Rourke continued. “There have been whispers. Concerns about anomalies in The Deep Cut. About power fluctuations. About stress patterns in the lower rings.” He shook his head, almost amused. “Let me reassure you: those concerns are outdated. The fluctuations were caused by a temporary alignment of mining drones during a refueling cycle. A hiccup. Not a flaw.”

On the screen, a director in a crimson sash from the Martian Trade Guild leaned forward. “Director Rourke, your confidence is noted. But the Authority has a mandate for safety as well as production. If there are irregularities—”

“There are no irregularities,” Rourke said, cutting him off with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “But I appreciate your diligence. That’s why we have Dr. Tarkov. Why we have our entire science division monitoring every fluctuation, every byte of telemetry. We are not just mining ore. We are mining *trust*.”

He turned slightly, gesturing to Tarkov. “Perhaps she can walk you through the latest audit findings?”

Tarkov stepped forward, slate in hand. Her voice was steady, but Rourke detected the faintest tremor beneath it.

“Director Rourke is correct that the mining alignment caused transient load shifts,” she said. “But the energy grid responded in a way that suggests an older configuration was still active in the secondary routing.”

A pause. The directors leaned in.

“An older configuration?” asked a woman from Europa, her brow furrowing.

“One that prioritizes node efficiency over system resilience,” Tarkov continued. “It’s a design choice. One that could lead to cascade failure under sustained stress.”

Rourke’s smile didn’t waver. “Dr. Tarkov is being cautious. As we all should be. But caution is not panic. And speculation is not fact.”

Tarkov’s thumb hovered over the slate. She didn’t look away from the screen.

“Sir,” she said, “the pattern matches the original non-standard grid used in the pre-commissioning phase. The one designed by the station’s original engineer.”

The deck went quiet. Even the hum of the ventilation seemed to pause.

Rourke’s fingers curled into his palm. He kept his face smooth. Implacable.

“That design was never implemented,” he said. “It was reviewed. Rejected. Replaced by the current standard grid. One we all trust.”

“But what if it wasn’t replaced?” Tarkov pressed. “What if parts of it were retained? Hidden?”

Rourke stepped closer to the camera, his voice dropping to a tone of intimate authority. “Nia, my dear, you’re suggesting that someone deliberately sabotaged the station’s safety protocols.”

“I’m suggesting someone designed a system that works beautifully under normal load, but fails predictably under crisis load. Someone who knew exactly where to place the weak points.”

The directors shifted in their seats. The woman from Europa spoke again. “Director Rourke, if there is even a possibility of structural compromise—”

“There is no possibility,” Rourke interrupted, sharper now. “Because I *built* this station. I signed off on every blueprint. I stand by every weld. This is not a question of design. It’s a question of *perception*.”

He turned to Tarkov. His voice was steel wrapped in velvet. “Dr. Tarkov, I appreciate your dedication. But I will not allow this station—*my* station—to be painted as unstable on the basis of theoretical anomalies. The audit next month will confirm what we already know: Corio is a marvel of modern engineering.”

Tarkov’s lips thinned. She didn’t argue. She didn’t push. But her fingers trembled as she tucked the slate under her arm.

Rourke turned back to the camera. “The next twenty minutes will feature a live tour of our refinery array. But first—a reminder. Corio Station doesn’t just extract minerals. It extracts *order* from chaos. And that order is what keeps us alive out here.”

The feed cut to a looping shot of the refinery belt rotating beneath Corio’s shadow. The directors appeared satisfied, nodding in unison.

Rourke didn’t wait for the transmission to end. He tapped the console, ending the feed himself. Silence rushed in.

Tarkov exhaled sharply. Then, softly: “You’ll regret this.”

He turned to her, arms loose at his sides. “Regret what, Doctor? Maintaining control? Or being wrong?”

She met his gaze. Held it. “You just dismissed a structural anomaly that matches a design only one person knew.”

Rourke leaned in slightly. “And that person doesn’t exist anymore. Not officially.”

Tarkov’s face darkened. “She still does. In the files. In the walls. In the ones who remember.”

Rourke crossed his arms. “Nia, if you’re trying to warn me that Linnea Voss is a ghost haunting this station, relax. She’s been erased. The files scrubbed. The blueprints overwritten. There’s no proof. Only paranoia.”

Tarkov’s voice dropped. “Then why am I the only one seeing the pattern?”

Rourke didn’t answer. The status board in the corner flashed green. The feed was gone. The directors were satisfied.

For now.

A tech assistant entered, clipboard in hand. “Sir, the refinery tour is ready. The directors’ shuttle is docking in ten.”

Rourke nodded. “Excellent. Nia, join us. Let’s show them a system that works.”

Tarkov hesitated. Then she fell into step beside him.

They walked down the corridor, the observation deck’s lights casting long shadows. The hum of the station wrapped around them like a shroud.

Rourke’s comm unit pinged. He tapped it.

“Director.”

A voice crackled through, strained. “Sir, we’ve got a situation in The Deep Cut. Atmospheric filter array 7 has gone offline. Pressure differential detected.”

Rourke paused mid-step. Tarkov stopped too. Her eyes widened.

“Repeat that,” Rourke said, voice low.

“Filter 7 failed. Pressure in Sector 12 is dropping. Crew is evacuating now.”

Tarkov’s breath hitched. “That’s in the lower hab ring. Lowest tolerance.”

Rourke’s jaw tightened. “Patch me through to Maintenance.”

A second later, Grit’s voice came over the line, rough with static. “Director, we got a cascade. Filter 7 overloaded. Blew a seal. We’re sealing the bulkhead now, but we lost containment.”

Tarkov’s hand flew to her mouth. Rourke’s pulse jumped.

“Casualties?” he asked.

“None yet. Grit out.”

The line went dead.

Rourke stared at his comm. The deck felt too still. Too cold.

Tarkov stepped closer. “That’s exactly the kind of failure I was warning you about.”

He turned to her, eyes burning. “Then you tell me, Doctor—what would *you* do?”

She met his gaze. “I’d find the person who designed the original grid. And I’d ask them how to fix it.”

Rourke didn’t answer. He turned and walked toward the lift, his stride quick, purposeful.

Tarkov followed, her mind racing.

The lift doors opened. They stepped inside.

As the doors sealed, Rourke spoke, voice barely above a growl.

“Get me the maintenance logs from the last three weeks. All of them. And pull up every anomaly report from The Deep Cut. Every one.”

Tarkov nodded, already tapping her slate.

The lift descended.

And as the station groaned around them, Rourke made a silent vow.

He would find the ghost in the machine.

Even if it killed him.

Chapter 5

The Deep Cut was a wound that never closed.

Grit braced his palm against the corroded hatch of the atmospheric filter array. The metal burned under his grip—too hot, or maybe that was just the way his blood felt. Maintenance teams swarmed the corridor outside Sector 12, their boots thudding against the grating, voices sharp with adrenaline. But in here, the air tasted like burnt ozone and old failure.

Rourke stood at the center of the breach, his polished boots planted on the deck plates like he was anchoring the whole damn station. “Status.”

Grit forced his fingers to uncurl. “Filter 7’s fried. Blew a primary conduit. Pressure’s holding at 0.7 atmospheres, but that’s a leak, not a seal.”

Rourke’s gaze flicked to the jagged tear in the bulkhead. “And the cascade?”

Grit didn’t look up from the damage report scrolling on his wrist slate. “Secondary filters are cycling hard. If they overload, we lose the whole ring.”

A woman in a maintenance rig—Kess, one of the new hires—adjusted the pressure regulator on her helmet. “We need to vent the sector and reroute through the lower loop.”

Rourke’s jaw worked. “Not until I say.”

Grit exhaled through his teeth. “Sir, we don’t have time for—”

“You don’t give orders, Marr.” Rourke’s voice cut across the bay. “You fix. You report. You *obey*.”

Grit felt the old familiar burn in his gut—the one that had nearly gotten him demoted years ago, the one Linnea had always talked him down from. He swallowed it.

“Then let me fix it,” he said. “I know the legacy protocol. The one she built into the grid.”

Rourke’s eyes narrowed. “The one that isn’t supposed to exist.”

Grit didn’t answer. He didn’t have to. The evidence was right here—hot metal, failing seals, and a station that shouldn’t be breathing this hard.

Rourke stepped closer. “Do it.”

Grit moved to the control console, his boots sticky with something he didn’t want to name. The screen flickered to life under his touch. He keyed in the override sequence—a long string of commands, stored in muscle memory from nights spent hunched over schematics with Linnea breathing down his neck.

The system accepted the input. Then rejected it.

Grit’s fingers paused. “It’s asking for retinal verification.”

Rourke didn’t hesitate. He leaned in, lined up his eye with the scanner. The system beeped once, twice—then rejected him.

A red light flared on the console.

Kess swore. “It’s locked to a retinal signature. Only one person’s.”

Rourke backed off, jaw tight. “Voss.”

Grit didn’t confirm it. He didn’t need to. They both knew.

Rourke turned on his heel. “Get her down here. Now.”

Grit’s stomach lurched. “You can’t drag her out of The Vault mid-crisis.”

“I can do whatever I want.” Rourke’s comm crackled. A voice—sharp, unfamiliar—cut through the static. “Director, we’ve got another cascade in the lower loop. Filters 3 and 5 are failing simultaneously.”

Rourke’s face went pale under the station’s sterile light. “Shut them down manually.”

“Can’t. The override grid’s active. We can’t touch the controls without retinal scan.”

A beat of silence. The kind that made Grit’s teeth ache.

Rourke’s voice dropped. “Bring me Voss. Or we lose the Cut.”

Grit reached for the console again, fingers flying. “Then let me run it. I’ve got her protocols memorized. The retinal’s a failsafe, not a lockout. The system just needs *someone* who trusts the design.”

Rourke’s glare could’ve stripped paint. “You?”

“I know how it works.” Grit forced the words out. “I helped build it.”

Rourke studied him for a long, suffocating moment. Then, with a sharp nod, he stepped aside. “Do it. Fast.”

Grit didn’t wait. He keyed the sequence, input his own retinal scan from memory. The system accepted it—this time. The filters cycled down, rerouted, stabilized. The pressure gauge ticked upward, creeping back toward safe.

Silence settled over the bay, thick and heavy.

Rourke exhaled through his nose. “Took you long enough.”

Grit wiped sweat from his brow with the back of his glove. “We got lucky.”

“Luck’s got nothing to do with it,” Rourke said. “It’s skill. Discipline. *Control*.”

Grit didn’t argue. He just watched the gauges, waiting for the next failure to crawl out of the dark.

Then it happened.

A high-pitched shriek split the air. The console’s alarm blared. Red warnings pulsed across the screen.

Grit lunged forward. “Override failed. Secondary cascade in Sector 11.”

Rourke’s face twisted. “Impossible.”

Kess pointed at the feed. “Look.”

On the monitor, black smoke billowed from a vent in the ceiling. The corridor lights flickered. Then died.

Emergency battens flared to life, casting the bay in a sickly orange glow.

Rourke’s comm spat static. “Marr—what the hell is happening?”

Grit’s hands flew over the controls. “The grid’s rerouting power to the damaged nodes. It’s trying to compensate.” He shook his head. “But it’s not your grid, Director. It’s *hers*.”

Rourke didn’t respond. He just stood there, watching the station fight a battle it wasn’t supposed to know existed.

Grit keyed the override again, faster this time. The system accepted his retinal. The cascade slowed. The smoke thinned.

Then, just as the pressure stabilized, the intercom crackled.

“Attention, Corio Station. This is Captain Jace Duvall, Security Oversight. We’ve detected unauthorized access in The Vault. Reroute all available security to the central spire. Repeat—”

Rourke’s head snapped toward the nearest guard. “Lock it down. Now.”

The guard hesitated. “Sir, who’s accessing—”

“*Now.*”

The guard ran.

Grit wiped grease from his hands onto his pants. The bay smelled like ozone and burning copper. “You think she’s here?”

Rourke didn’t answer. He was already striding toward the exit, boots ringing against the deck.

Grit followed, Kess trailing behind. The corridor outside was chaos—crew sprinting, alarms howling, the station groaning like a living thing in pain.

Rourke ignored it all. He turned to Grit as they reached the lift. “She’s going to pay for this.”

Grit didn’t answer. He just stepped into the lift, the weight of the station pressing down on him like a tombstone.

The doors sealed. The lift lurched upward.

Then Grit muttered to Kess, low enough that Rourke wouldn’t hear.

“This never happened under Linnea’s watch.”

Rourke’s shoulders stiffened. But he didn’t turn around.

The lift climbed. The alarms didn’t stop.

And somewhere in the dark, Linnea Voss was watching.

Chapter 6

The Vault was designed to intimidate.

Linnea knew. She’d helped design it.

The circular chamber gleamed under cold white light, every surface polished to a mirror sheen. Blueprints of Corio Station hung suspended in holographic display at the center, rotating slowly like some corporate shrine. The directors sat in a semicircle of chrome and leather, their voices bouncing off the vaulted ceiling. Rourke stood at the podium, his back to the massive blast door, blocking the only exit Linnea could see.

She stood three meters behind the last row, fingers curled around a maintenance access terminal embedded in the wall. The device was a relic—one of the original test panels from Corio’s construction. Rourke had ordered it upgraded, but the hardware still carried the old palm print scanner. Hers.

A flicker of movement caught her eye. Kess, one of the new maintenance hires, passed through the outer corridor. Her helmet light flashed a quick acknowledgment—*I’m covering for you.* Linnea gave no sign she’d seen.

Rourke adjusted his cuffs. “The latest export projections are down again. Earth is threatening sanctions if we don’t meet quota by next cycle.”

Director Harkin, a gaunt man with sunken eyes, leaned forward. “And the structural concerns?”

“Under control.” Rourke’s voice was smooth, rehearsed. “Our new reinforcement protocols have eliminated all stress anomalies.”

Linnea’s thumb hovered over the biometric pad. The terminal hummed, hungry for a signature.

Director Chen, a woman with silver-streaked hair, tapped her datapad. “Then why is morale still dropping? Crew bonuses are two weeks late. People are talking.”

Rourke’s smile didn’t reach his eyes. “Morale is a variable. Production is not.”

Linnea exhaled through her nose. The terminal beeped once—ready.

She depressed the scanner.

The device chirped, green. A prompt appeared: *Retinal verification required.*

She leaned in. The scanner blinked twice. Then accepted.

A data stream lit up her wrist slate—anonymous, untraceable, routed through three firewalls she’d built before Corio even had a name. She selected the bonus allocation file, hit execute, and watched as five hundred thousand credits split across two hundred accounts.

She closed the terminal before the first alert could sound.

Rourke droned on. “Any further questions?”

Chen frowned. “Director, your terminal—”

A soft chime echoed through the Vault’s speakers. Heads turned.

Rourke’s executive display lit up. A notification flashed in red: *Unauthorized Allocation Detected – Sector 7 Payroll.*

His jaw tightened. He tapped the screen. Another notification: *System Override – Source: Legacy Protocol Alpha.*

Linnea’s fingers twitched. She pressed her palm against the wall, anchoring herself.

Rourke’s voice cut through the murmurs. “What the hell is this?”

Harkin leaned toward his display. “It’s a bonus payout. Just went through.”

Chen’s eyes flicked to Rourke’s face. “Unofficial?”

Rourke’s fingers flew. “Unauthorized. Someone—”

Linnea stepped forward. “Director Rourke.”

Every head turned.

She walked to the front, boots clicking against the deck. Rourke’s gaze locked onto her like a predator scenting prey.

“You wanted transparency?” She gestured to his terminal. “There it is. Crews get paid. Morale stabilizes. Production doesn’t slip.”

Rourke’s lips parted. “You.”

She didn’t flinch. “I’m the only one who still remembers what it’s like to actually run this station.”

Chen exhaled. “She’s not wrong. The numbers look good.”

Harkin rubbed his temples. “If it’s coming from an OBA account, it should be traceable.”

Linnea shook her head. “It’s not OBA funds. It’s private capital. Untaxed. Unregulated.” She met Rourke’s stare. “And it’s not the first time.”

A muscle jumped in Rourke’s jaw. He tapped his comm. “Security. I want every anonymous allocation in the last three months audited. Now.”

Linnea didn’t break eye contact. Inside, something cold and sharp uncoiled.

Rourke turned back to the room. “This meeting is adjourned.”

The directors filed out, shoulders tense, whispers rising like steam. Rourke didn’t move. Neither did Linnea.

Then, from the doorway, Chen paused. “Director, you might want to check the structural integrity logs before your next audit. Some of the stress reports look… unusual.”

Rourke’s voice was steel. “Leave.”

The door hissed shut.

Silence.

Rourke stepped closer, his polished shoes clicking. “You think you can destabilize my authority with pocket change?”

Linnea didn’t smile. “I think you’re forgetting who built this station.”

“You built *your* station.” His hand twitched toward his pistol. “This one belongs to me.”

She held her ground. “Then explain why the filters in The Deep Cut just cycled on legacy protocols last night. Protocols only I know.”

Rourke’s face went still. “You were there.”

“I’m always there.” She tilted her head. “You just didn’t notice until it was too late.”

His comm crackled. A voice, strained: “Director, we’ve got another cascade in The Deep Cut. Filter array 9’s ruptured. Pressure dropping.”

Rourke didn’t look away from Linnea. “Contain it.”

“Can’t. The override grid’s active again. Needs retinal scan.”

Rourke’s eyes narrowed. “Why didn’t I know about this?”

Linnea didn’t answer. She already knew what he didn’t.

The station was protecting itself.

And it knew her voice.

Rourke’s comm squawked again. “Sir, it’s getting worse. We need the retinal now.”

He finally broke eye contact, barking into the comm. “Lock it down! I’ll be there in—”

A shadow moved in the corridor outside. Captain Jace Duvall stepped into the Vault, his security jacket unzipped at the collar, his face set in a way that made Linnea’s stomach tighten.

He looked at Rourke. Then at her.

His voice was low. “Director. We’ve got a situation in The Deep Cut.”

Rourke’s fingers curled into fists. “Not now, Duvall.”

Jace ignored him. His gaze stayed on Linnea. “There’s a crack in the bulkhead. One of the primary stress nodes. It wasn’t supposed to fail.”

A beat.

Linnea’s pulse spiked. “Where?”

“Sector 11. The old reinforcement grid.”

Her breath caught. She knew that node. She’d designed it to withstand 1.8 atmospheres. Rourke’s “improvements” had downgraded it to 1.2.

Jace’s jaw worked. “We’re running a drill in twenty.”

Rourke finally turned away, striding toward the exit. “Then run it. Fast.”

Jace didn’t move. He watched Linnea—really watched her—for the first time in months. His voice dropped to a murmur. “You know why it’s failing.”

She didn’t deny it. Couldn’t. The proof was in the crack spreading through Corio’s bones.

Rourke barked from the doorway. “Duvall! Move.”

Jace hesitated. Then he was gone, boots thudding against the deck.

Linnea stood alone in The Vault, surrounded by ghosts.

The station groaned in the distance—a sound like a held breath finally released.

And somewhere below, metal began to bend.

Chapter 7

The Deep Cut never slept.

Grit tubes hummed overhead, casting a sickly yellow glow over the maintenance floor. Crews moved in practiced rhythm, calibrating pressure valves, logging torque readings. The air tasted thin—overworked filters, stale sweat, the metallic tang of recycled oxygen.

Linnea stood at the edge of the safety drill staging area, arms crossed. She should’ve been in The Vault. She should’ve been anywhere but here. But Rourke had made his power move, and now the station was bleeding.

Jace arrived without announcement.

One second the bay was empty of supervisors. The next, he was there, boot clamped over a loose floor plate, testing its give with his heel.

Linnea didn’t look up. “You’re late.”

Jace didn’t flinch. “Traffic at the Hub. Rourke wanted a full tour of the audit trail.”

“Wonderful use of time.”

He crouched, fingers brushing the deck. “You ever notice how he always tours when something’s wrong?”

Linnea finally met his eyes. “Only because he likes the look on our faces when we hide the truth.”

Jace exhaled through his nose. “Today’s drill’s going to run hot.”

“It always runs hot.”

“Not like this.” He stood, wiped his palms on his thighs. “Higher pressure. Full node stress. Whole point’s to see if the new alloy skins hold under load.”

Linnea chewed her lip. “They won’t.”

Jace tilted his head. “You sound sure.”

“I designed the skins. I know their limits.”

A pause. Then, softer: “Then why’s it happening?”

She almost laughed. Because Rourke had swapped out the carbon-weave for a cheaper molybdenum alloy. Because he’d cut corners on every load-bearing joint in The Deep Cut. Because she’d spent seven years building a station that could survive the void—and he’d spent seven years erasing her from it.

But she said none of that.

Instead, she nodded toward the staging console. “Hit start. Let’s see the damage.”

Jace keyed the drill into motion. The bay lights dimmed. Emergency beacons pulsed red. A voice, synthetic and calm, filled the chamber: “Safety drill commencing in T-minus sixty seconds. All personnel brace for structural load.”

Linnea braced against a stanchion. The deck trembled—not the drill’s usual simulation, but something deeper, a harmonic groan from the station’s spine. She watched the pressure gauges climb. 0.9. 1.1. 1.4.

Then the crack.

It wasn’t loud. Not at first. A hiss, like air escaping a pinhole. Then the deck plates around Grid 4-7 shifted. A seam split. A thin white line traced itself across the alloy, spidering outward.

The drill siren wailed.

Jace was already running. “Lock it down! Seal the sector!”

The crew scrambled. Tools clattered. Someone shouted into a comm. Linnea stayed where she was, eyes locked on the fracture. It was too precise. Too clean. Not a failure of material. A failure of design.

Someone bumped her shoulder. Kess. Face streaked with grease. “Boss… was this designed like this?”

The question hung.

Linnea didn’t answer. She didn’t have to.

Kess turned to Grit, who stood frozen beside a fallen wrench. “Elias. You hear me? Was this designed like Linnea’s specs?”

Grit’s Adam’s apple bobbed. His hands shook. Then he nodded—once, slow. “Yeah. That’s how she drew it.”

The words hung.

Jace skidded back, boots skidding on scattered parts. “Grit, what the hell—”

Grit didn’t look at him. His gaze stayed on Linnea. “You lied to me. You said you never—”

She cut him off. “Not now.”

Jace’s face darkened. “Linnea. What the hell is going on?”

She ignored him. Focused on the fracture. It was widening. Breathing. The station was alive. And it was hurting.

Kess stepped closer. “Boss, we need to evacuate.”

Linnea finally looked at Jace. Really looked. His jaw was tight. His eyes—sharp. Calculating. He wasn’t just a security captain here. He was a man who’d been handed a puzzle and asked to solve it without knowing half the pieces.

She said, quietly, “This station wasn’t built to fail.”

Jace’s fingers twitched. “Then why is it?”

“Because someone rebuilt it wrong.”

A beat. Then Grit’s voice, raw: “You knew. All this time.”

Linnea didn’t deny it. Couldn’t.

Jace’s comm crackled. A voice, tight with static: “Captain, we’ve got secondary fractures in Sector 13. Pressure drop in The Deep Cut. We need to seal the grid.”

Jace didn’t move. His eyes stayed on Linnea. “Last chance. What do you know?”

She exhaled. Short. Sharp. “I know this station remembers my voice.”

A flicker. Then comprehension.

Jace’s comm squawked again. “Sir!”

He finally looked away, barking into the device. “On my way.” He turned back to Linnea. “You’re coming with me.”

She didn’t argue.

As they moved toward the exit, the deck plates groaned again—deeper this time. A warning. A pulse. The station was speaking. And it wasn’t asking.

Chapter 8

The Hub smelled like burnt circuit boards and adrenaline.

Silvano Rourke stood in the center of the observation deck, palms flat on the reinforced glass, watching the Deep Cut glow red through the emergency scanners. His reflection in the viewport didn’t flinch. Neither did his voice. “Patch the leak. Now.”

A director from Earth—some suit with a nameplate that read *Mendes*—shifted his weight. “The coolant isn’t just leaking. It’s saturated the archive floor. We’re losing integrity on the lower stacks.”

Rourke didn’t turn. “Then reroute.”

“Not that simple. The Vault’s atmospheric controls are offline. We’ve got forty-seven minutes before the data chafes.”

Forty-seven minutes. The number settled in Rourke’s gut like a failed injection. Almost twice the time he’d given Tarkov to explain herself.

Dr. Nia Tarkov stood three paces behind him, arms locked behind her back, spine straight as a pressure rod. Her eyes tracked the flickering screens, not the man beside her. “Director, the anomaly isn’t just a fluctuation. It’s a signature. One I’ve seen before—in fluid dynamics, not power grids.”

Rourke finally faced her. “Then explain why your last report said the station was stable.”

“Because it was—I thought.” She tapped the console beside her. A data stream flashed: *Gamma spike in Grid 3. Origin: non-standard energy feed.* “This isn’t a system glitch. It’s an override. Someone’s tapping the original grid.”

A beat of silence. Too long. The kind that crackled with impending order collapse.

Rourke’s comm buzzed. He thumbed it without glancing at the screen. “Status.”

Captain Duvall’s voice came tight, controlled. “Sir, we’ve got a breach in The Vault’s retinal archive. Scan shows unauthorized access.”

Rourke’s fingers twitched on the device. “When?”

“Less than two minutes ago. Retinal match flagged as—”

“Linnea Voss,” he finished.

Tarkov’s head snapped up. “Linnea’s in the Vault?”

Rourke ignored her. “Shut the archive down. Seal the deck. I want the retinal logs scrubbed from memory.”

Duvall didn’t hesitate. “Already in progress. But sir—”

“Yes?”

“There’s more. Tarkov’s last research dump hit the OBA faction channels. They’re calling for an emergency audit of your structural oversight.”

The suit from Earth—Mendes—let out a low whistle. “That’s not just a glitch, Director. That’s mutiny.”

Rourke’s jaw tightened. “It’s a distraction.”

Tarkov stepped forward, eyes alight with something between fear and fury. “Distraction from what?”

“From the fact that someone just stole proof of Corio’s original design.”

Mendes paled. “You’re saying Voss is still inside the station? After all this time?”

Rourke didn’t answer. He was already moving, boots clicking against the deck as he headed for the emergency stairwell. The Hub’s alarms wailed in his wake.

—

Linnea didn’t hear the alarms.

She was too busy listening to the station scream.

The Deep Cut’s maintenance corridor hummed with panic—crew shouting, tools clattering, the distant hiss of failing seals. She moved against the current, fingers brushing the wall-mounted diagnostic panels. Her retinal scan still worked. The system recognized her. *Linnea Voss. Clearance: Level 9. Access: Vault Archive.*

Good.

The Vault wasn’t just a room anymore. It was a wound. Emergency lights cast jagged shadows across the bulkhead where the archival stacks had once stood. Now, the floor was slick with coolant, data rods glinting like fish in a dying tide. And in the center of the mess, a single, familiar data chip lay half-buried in the muck.

Her schematics. Still here. Still waiting.

She crouched, plucking it from the grime. The casing was warm. Alive. The station’s pulse thrummed through the metal.

A shadow fell over her.

Linnea didn’t look up. “Grit. I should’ve known you’d be here.”

Elias Marr stood over her, arms crossed. His face was a map of exhaustion and something else—guilt, maybe. Or regret. “You always were too sentimental about the past.”

She wiped the chip on her sleeve. “Sentimental? I’m retrieving what’s mine.”

“It’s not yours anymore.” His voice dropped. “Rourke’s got the whole OBA breathing down his neck. Tarkov’s data’s everywhere. Someone’s going to put two and two together.”

Linnea stood, chip clutched tight. “Not if I’m not here to add it.”

Grit’s eyes flicked to the emergency exit. Then to the retinal scanner. “You’re not thinking of—”

“I’m thinking of not dying in a station he rebuilt wrong.” She stepped toward the scanner. “This is my station. My voice. My design.”

“And what happens when Rourke figures out you’ve been here the whole time?” Grit grabbed her wrist. Not hard. Just enough to anchor her. “You think he’ll just let you walk away?”

Linnea kept her gaze on the scanner. “He already did.”

The retinal scan locked. The Vault door hissed open.

Grit didn’t move. “Linnea. Listen to me. You can’t outrun this. Not anymore.”

She stepped into the archive’s mouth. “Watch me.”

—

The Hub’s atrium was chaos.

Rourke strode through the crowd, his comm alight with updates. *Secondary breach in Sector 11. Pressure stabilizers offline. Tarkov detained pending review.*

He ignored the shouts, the panicked faces, the way the station itself seemed to tremble under the weight of its own mistakes. Mendes jogged beside him, breathless. “Director, the faction’s demanding your resignation. They’re calling for an independent structural review of the entire colony.”

Rourke’s comm buzzed again. He thumbed it. “Duvall.”

Jace’s voice came through, strained. “Sir, I’ve got a situation in the Hub. Linnea Voss just accessed The Vault.”

Rourke stopped mid-step. The atrium’s noise faded. “Full access?”

“Retinal and voiceprint. She took something.”

A cold certainty settled in Rourke’s chest. “Find her.”

“Already on it.” A pause. Then, quieter: “She’s not hiding.”

Rourke’s fingers curled into a fist. “She doesn’t have to.”

Mendes leaned in. “What’s happening?”

Rourke looked at the man, really looked at him. Then he said, “The station’s bleeding. And Linnea just pulled the knife.”

He turned toward the emergency stairwell, already calculating the fastest route to the Deep Cut.

The chip in his pocket felt like a live grenade.

Chapter 9

The Vault door sealed behind Linnea with a hiss of equalizing pressure. She stood in the dim corridor of The Vault’s outer ring, the chip warm in her palm. The station’s alarms still wailed in the distance, but here, in the archive’s quiet shadow, her pulse was the only rhythm.

A hand clamped over her shoulder.

She turned.

Silvano Rourke stood there, his tailored jacket dusted with coolant residue, his expression unreadable. No anger. No triumph. Just the quiet certainty of a man who’d already won.

“You’re going to get yourself killed,” he said.

Linnea tucked the chip into her pocket. “I’ve survived worse.”

Rourke stepped closer, close enough that she caught the faint metallic tang of his cologne beneath the station’s acrid undertones. “Not like this. Not when the whole colony is about to fracture on live feeds.”

She crossed her arms. “Then you should’ve built it right the first time.”

A flicker crossed his face—something raw, something human—but it vanished before Linnea could name it. “I did what needed doing. You know that. Corio was never going to survive Earth’s oversight. Not with their safety margins and cost reports.”

“So you stole it.” Her voice was flat. “You stole my name, my work, my life.”

“And you walked away.” He tilted his head. “You took the severance, the credits, the quiet life. You decided the fight wasn’t worth it.”

Linnea exhaled through her nose. “I decided the station was worth more than my pride.”

Rourke’s comm buzzed. He thumbed it without looking down. “Director.”

Captain Jace Duvall’s voice filled the corridor, terse and controlled. “Sir, we’ve got a containment breach in Grid 3. Atmospheric regulators are failing. Need authorization to reroute power.”

Rourke didn’t blink. “Do it. Use the auxiliary feeds.”

“Already in progress. But sir—there’s another issue. Station security just detained Dr. Tarkov in Sector 7. She was trying to access restricted schematics.”

The air between them thickened.

Linnea felt the shift in Rourke before he spoke. His voice dropped, low and deliberate. “And what was she looking for?”

Jace hesitated. “Something related to Grid 3’s original design.”

Rourke’s jaw tightened. Linnea watched the muscle work in his cheek. He was calculating. Always calculating.

He turned back to her. “You know what she was after.”

Linnea said nothing.

Rourke stepped closer, close enough that she could see the faint scar above his left eyebrow—the one from the orbital construction accident seven years ago. The one he’d tried to claim was his own.

“Here’s how this ends,” he said. “You come back. Full pardon. Restored title. Your name reinstated. You validate the changes I’ve made. You save this station from collapse.” He spread his hands. “Simple.”

Linnea laughed. Not a sound of joy. Of defeat.

“That’s not an offer,” she said. “That’s a plea.”

Rourke’s fingers twitched. “It’s a chance.”

“For who?” She shook her head. “You’ve spent seven years erasing me. Seven years taking credit for my work. Seven years letting people die because your ‘improvements’ cut corners. And now you need me to fix it?” She stepped toward the nearest exit. “Too late.”

Rourke’s comm buzzed again. Not Jace this time. Someone else. A name flashed on the screen—*N.Tarkov – Detention*. He thumbed it. “What is it?”

A voice crackled through, strained. “Director, we’ve got a situation in Detention Block 4. Dr. Tarkov’s attempting to transmit data. We’ve got a hard lock, but—”

Rourke’s hand shot out, gripping Linnea’s wrist. Not hard. Just enough to stop her.

She looked down at his fingers. Then up at him.

“You’re detaining her for what?” Linnea asked.

“For theft.” His thumb brushed the inside of her wrist—once, deliberately. A man making a point. “She accessed restricted schematics. Attempted to leak them to Earth factions.”

Linnea’s pulse jumped. Not from fear. From recognition.

She knew those schematics.

She’d written them.

Rourke leaned in, his voice dropping to a growl. “You know what she was trying to do. She was going to expose the flaws. Expose *you*. And now she’s trapped.”

Linnea stared at him. The man who had stolen her life. The man who thought he could still control her.

“You’re not detaining her for theft,” Linnea said. “You’re detaining her for knowing too much.”

Rourke didn’t deny it.

Linnea shook her head. “You really think restoring my title changes anything? That I’d stand at your side and lie for you? After everything?”

“No.” He released her. “I think you’d stand there and watch the truth come out. And that’s almost as good.”

She turned toward the exit.

“Linnea.”

She paused.

“This station is failing. Not because of the alloy skins. Not because of the filters. Because someone *sabotaged the original grid*. Someone added pressure points. Weaknesses. Someone who knew exactly where to strike to make the whole thing collapse under stress.”

Linnea didn’t face him. But she felt his gaze burn into her back.

“And I need to know,” Rourke said, “if that someone was you.”

She walked away.

The corridor lights flickered. The station groaned around them. Somewhere, an alarm wailed.

Rourke’s comm buzzed again.

This time, he didn’t answer. He just watched her go.

Chapter 10

The maintenance alcove in The Deep Cut smelt of rust and recirculated air. Linnea knelt behind the access panel, her fingers numb despite the gloves. The hidden cavity yawned open like a mouth full of sharp metal teeth. She slid her left hand into the slot, palm up. The retinal scanner flickered green once, twice—then locked.

A hush fell over the station. Not the usual hum of life support and machinery. This was the quiet before something broke.

Her thumb hovered over the activation stud. Seven years of ledgers sat on the encrypted slate behind that panel. Every unauthorized credit transfer. Every structural fix logged under dummy work orders. Every lie she’d fed to Earth’s auditors to keep Corio breathing.

She exhaled.

The stud clicked.

The slate’s screen flared to life. Her face stared back—older, wearier, but unmistakable. The data loaded in a cascade of numbers and schematics. At the top, a single line pulsed like a heartbeat:

*Authorized: Linnea Voss*

She should have felt vindication. Instead, her throat tightened. This wasn’t triumph. It was proof.

A chime sounded in the corridor.

Linnea’s spine locked. That tone—private, unscreened. Only one person had that frequency.

Jace.

She killed the slate’s glow, dropped it into her pack, and zipped the panel shut just as footsteps echoed around the bend. Fast. Deliberate. Not the shuffling gait of maintenance crews.

She stayed kneeling, back to the wall, heartbeat loud in her ears.

Jace rounded the corner, black uniform crisp, face half in shadow. His eyes locked onto the access panel.

“Linnea.”

She didn’t move. “Captain.”

He stopped three meters away. The station’s flickering lights turned his jawline into a series of jagged shadows. “You’re in The Deep Cut after hours. Again.”

“And you’re in The Deep Cut after hours tracking me.” She tilted her head toward the panel. “Again.”

Jace didn’t smile. Neither did she.

“Rourke’s office,” he said. “Now.”

“No.” Linnea stood, dusting her knees. “I’m not walking into another meeting where he pats himself on the back while the struts groan.”

Jace’s gaze flicked to the panel behind her, then back. “You know something about Grid 3’s failure?”

“Only what you do.” She stepped forward, forcing him to sidestep or block her. He chose the latter.

His hand came up—not to grab, not to strike. Just a barrier. “You’re obstructing an OBA investigation.”

“Am I?” She stopped within arm’s reach. “Or am I the only one who knows Grid 3 wasn’t built to take this kind of load?”

Jace’s jaw tightened. “That’s classified.”

“So’s my name on the original blueprints.” She tapped her chest above her badge. “You ever wonder why my ID says ‘Chief Structural Integrity Engineer’ but the credits for Corio’s design go to Rourke?”

His fingers twitched. “Linnea—”

“Don’t.” She cut him off with a single word, sharp as a blade. “Don’t call me by my first name like we’re still colleagues.”

A muscle jumped in his cheek. “We’re not?”

“Not since you started taking his orders.” She turned toward the corridor. “Walk with me. Or don’t. But I’m not going to Rourke’s office.”

Jace didn’t move. She took a step. He stayed rooted.

Then—

His comm buzzed. The sound cut through the silence like a blade.

Jace thumbed it. “Duvall.”

A voice crackled through, tinny and urgent. “Captain, we’ve got atmospheric regulators offline in Sector 3. Containment breach imminent.”

Jace’s eyes never left Linnea. “ETA?”

“Sixty seconds. We need you to reroute power from the secondary grid, but the override panel’s locked down.”

Linnea exhaled through her nose. Secondary grid—painted in red on every schematic she’d ever seen. Rourke’s “optimization” had dropped maintenance protocols to cut costs.

She said nothing.

Jace’s voice dropped. “I’ll be there in two.”

He killed the comm. Looked at Linnea.

She raised an eyebrow. “You’re leaving me here?”

“You know the override sequence.” His tone was flat, rehearsed. “You built half those overrides.”

“Built them. Didn’t sign off on them when Rourke cut every safety redundancy to meet Earth’s quarterly margins.”

Jace’s fingers curled into a fist. For a heartbeat, she thought he might grab her. Might shake her. Might do something reckless.

Instead, he stepped aside. “Then go fix it.”

Linnea moved past him, shoulder brushing his arm. The contact lasted a second. Barely. But it sent a charge through her chest she refused to name.

She didn’t look back. Didn’t watch him stride away down the corridor. Didn’t give him the satisfaction of seeing her hesitate.

By the time she reached the maintenance hub, the overhead lights pulsed in triple beats—station-wide alert. Breach imminent. Sixty seconds.

She keyed the override panel, fingers flying. The access code refused her the first time. She tried again. This time it accepted.

Rourke’s voice crackled over the comms, smooth and unconcerned. “All hands, brace for venting in Sector 3. Standard protocol.”

Linnea’s hands froze over the controls.

Standard protocol?

There was no standard protocol when the secondary grid failed. Not with the alloy skins already fractured from thermal cycling.

She rerouted the power manually—twisting the system into a configuration no engineer had ever approved. It would burn out two relays. It might trip the backup fail-safes.

But it would buy them time.

Her comm activated on its own. No ping. No warning. Just her screen flashing white.

A voice she knew.

Jace.

“Linnea.”

She didn’t answer.

“You’re still in The Deep Cut.”

She didn’t deny it.

“There’s no breach in Sector 3.”

The words hit like a physical blow. She turned, back against the panel, eyes on the corridor behind her.

Jace stood ten meters away, comm in hand. His face was pale under the emergency lighting.

“Then why—” her voice cracked “—why did the alert sound?”

He lifted the comm. Showed her the screen.

*AUTOMATIC RESPONSE ACTIVATED – FALSE ALERT ENGAGED*
*SOURCE: OVERRIDE PANEL – DEEP CUT*

Her blood turned to ice.

Someone had triggered the breach alert. Someone had forced the system to scream *emergency* for one purpose only—to get her here.

To trap her.

Jace took a step forward. “Linnea.”

She dropped into a crouch, fingers flying over the console once more. This time she disabled the override entirely. The panel gave a final groan, then went dark.

Silence.

Jace stood frozen. “What the hell did you just do?”

She stood, brushed dust from her knees. “I disconnected the failsafe Rourke installed to keep me quiet.”

Jace’s face changed. Not anger. Not fear. Something like betrayal.

“You think I’m working for him.”

She met his eyes. “Are you?”

He didn’t answer.

Her comm pinged again. Not Jace. Not an alert.

A personal channel.

Unknown ID. Encrypted.

She thumbed it open.

Static. Then—

“Linnea.”

Her breath stopped.

The voice was synthetic. Distorted.

But she knew it.

Her own voice.

From seven years ago.

*“If you ever need to disappear, use the secondary grid bypass in The Deep Cut. I built it in. No one else knows.”*

Silence.

Her screen went black.

She looked up at Jace.

His eyes were wide. His comm still in hand. Unaware he’d just heard his own voice.

Linnea’s hands trembled.

That message hadn’t been for her.

It had been for someone else.

Someone who had just realized the truth.

And that someone was standing in front of her now.

Chapter 11

The observation deck clung to the outer edge of Corio Hub, a transparent bubble suspended over the abyss of Jupiter’s gravity well. The viewport curved like a crescent moon, streaked with plasma burns from passing refinery slag. Rourke stood at the center, hands clasped behind his back, watching Earthrise over the Jovian horizon.

Linnea stepped into the deck behind him, boots silent on the grated floor. The air smelled of ionized metal and polished glass. No one else was present—no aides, no security. Just the two of them and the void.

Rourke didn’t turn. “You’re late.”

“You invited me here.” Her voice was steady. “I came.”

“After avoiding three summons.” He finally faced her, his smile thin. “Bold. Or careless.”

Linnea walked to the viewport. Jupiter’s banded face filled the glass, vast and indifferent. “I don’t answer to you anymore.”

“You answer to the OBA.” He stepped closer. “And I am the OBA.”

She exhaled through her nose. “You’re a thief with a title.”

Rourke didn’t flinch. “Your station is collapsing. Credits are bleeding. And you’re still playing the martyr.”

“My station?” She turned, eyes locking onto his. “You mean the structure I designed? The one you stole, then sabotaged to hide your theft?”

His jaw twitched. “Careful.”

“Why?” She spread her arms. “The truth is already unraveling. Your chief aide resigned this morning. Submitted a resignation letter citing ‘falsified audit reports’ and ‘structural negligence.’ You think that’s a coincidence?”

Rourke’s fingers curled. “He’ll be dealt with.”

“He was dealt with the moment he saw the ledgers.” Linnea tapped her thigh. “Every credit transfer. Every safety waiver. Every lie you signed in my name.”

Silence stretched between them, brittle as glass.

Then Rourke laughed. A dry, humorless sound. “You still think this is about money? Corio Station was never about credits. It was about control.”

“Control?” Linnea stepped forward. “You call starving the station of maintenance *control*? Cutting the alloy skins thinner than spec? Overloading the grids until they fracture? That’s not control. That’s suicide.”

Rourke’s eyes darkened. “The station holds. It always holds.”

“Because I’ve been patching it together.” Her voice cracked once. She steadied it. “I’ve been bleeding my own accounts dry to keep the struts from snapping. Every dollar you think you’ve saved by gutting the safety protocols? It’s blood money. And Earth’s auditors are about to find out.”

Rourke’s comm buzzed. He thumbed it without looking. “Yes?”

A voice, tinny and urgent: “Director, we’ve got a situation in The Vault. Someone’s accessing archived structural logs from the original build.”

Rourke’s gaze flicked to Linnea. “Who?”

“Access code matches Linnea Voss. Retinal and voiceprint confirmed.”

Rourke killed the comm. His fingers flexed. “You really thought you could hide forever?”

Linnea didn’t answer. She didn’t need to.

Rourke’s comm buzzed again. He ignored it.

“You’ll be arrested,” he said. “Charged with sabotage. Unauthorized access. Theft of OBA property.”

“And when the audit hits?” She tilted her head. “When they find the original schematics—the ones with my name on them? The ones that prove every ‘flaw’ in Corio’s design was intentional? What then?”

Rourke’s face went still.

Linnea smiled. “You’ll be the one in the dock. Not me.”

A chime echoed through the deck. The observation deck’s door irised open.

Nia Tarkov stood there, chest heaving, eyes wide. “Director, we’ve got another problem.”

Rourke’s voice was steel. “What?”

Tarkov’s eyes darted to Linnea. “Power fluctuations in Grid 3. Ones only someone familiar with Linnea’s non-standard energy grid could explain.”

Linnea felt the words like a blade between her ribs. She didn’t react. Didn’t flinch.

Rourke’s comm buzzed again. He ignored it.

“Enough,” he said to Tarkov. “Take her to the brig.”

Tarkov didn’t move.

Rourke’s eyes snapped to her. “Now.”

Tarkov swallowed. “Director, the fluctuations—they’re not natural. They’re a signature. A pattern. One that matches the original design specs before they were edited.”

Linnea’s pulse roared in her ears.

Rourke’s face darkened. “You too?”

Tarkov didn’t back down. “She’s telling the truth. And so am I.”

Rourke’s comm buzzed a fourth time. He thumbed it, held it to his ear.

Static. Then a voice—calm, distant.

“Director Rourke. This is Director Voss of Earth Oversight.”

Linnea’s breath stopped.

Rourke’s face went slack.

The voice continued. “I’m reviewing Corio Station’s latest integrity audit. I have to say, I’m surprised by the discrepancies. Especially the ones involving safety overrides that suspiciously match the original design specs. One with a retinal and voiceprint signature matching Linnea Voss.”

Rourke’s hand trembled. He killed the comm.

Silence.

Linnea didn’t smile. Didn’t gloat.

She just waited.

Rourke’s comm buzzed again. He thumbed it, held it to his ear.

For a long moment, he didn’t speak. Then—

“Resigned?” His voice was hollow. “Automatically?”

The viewport behind Linnea flickered. The station’s lights dimmed for a single heartbeat, then steadied.

Rourke lowered the comm. His face was gray.

Linnea stepped forward. “Congratulations, Director. You’ve just lost everything.”

Rourke didn’t look at her. His eyes were fixed on the viewport, on Jupiter’s endless storms.

The observation deck’s door irised open again.

Jace Duvall stood in the doorway, black uniform crisp, face unreadable. His eyes locked onto Linnea.

She didn’t look away.

Rourke’s comm buzzed a sixth time. He thumbed it. Listened. Then killed the comm.

He turned to Jace. “Captain, take her into custody.”

Jace didn’t move. “Sir, I have to report—”

“Now.” Rourke’s voice was a whipcrack.

Jace hesitated. Just for a second. Then he stepped forward.

Linnea didn’t resist. She let Jace’s hand close around her arm, firm but not cruel. His grip burned through her sleeve.

Rourke’s comm buzzed again.

Jace’s eyes flicked to the device. His jaw tightened.

Linnea watched him. Waiting.

Jace thumbed the comm. Listened. Then—

“Sir,” he said quietly. “It’s Earth Oversight. They’re requesting your immediate resignation. They have the evidence. All of it.”

Rourke’s face crumpled.

Jace didn’t look at Linnea. But his grip on her arm didn’t loosen.

The observation deck was silent except for the hum of the station’s failing stabilizers.

Linnea exhaled.

Then Jace’s comm activated.

A single chime.

He thumbed it open.

No voice. No static.

Just a screen flaring to life.

A face appeared.

Linnea’s face.

Eyes dark. Mouth set.

The words pulsed beneath:

*You’ve been looking for me.*

Jace’s grip on Linnea’s arm tightened.

His eyes met hers.

And for the first time, Linnea realized—he knew.
