Chapter 13

---

The transfer deck was cold. The air scrubbers wheezed, cycling through the same stale oxygen. Linnea stood between two OBA enforcers, wrists sealed in magnetized cuffs. The cuffs hummed faintly, magnets locking her bones in place.

Rourke stood ten meters away, flanked by armed guards. His jacket hung open. His shirt was wrinkled. He looked like a man who had aged in the last thirty minutes.

Jace stepped into the deck from the service corridor. His boots thudded on the grated floor. He didn’t meet Linnea’s eyes at first. He spoke to Rourke.

“Sir, Earth Oversight is sending a retrieval team. They’re docking in twelve minutes.”

Rourke’s jaw tightened. “And?”

“They’ve requested we hold all current personnel for questioning.” Jace’s voice was flat. “Including you.”

Rourke didn’t move. “And Linnea?”

Jace finally looked at her. His eyes were unreadable. “They didn’t mention her.”

Linnea felt the cuffs press into her skin. The magnets were calibrated to pinch, not cut. Not yet.

Rourke exhaled through his nose. “You’re taking her into custody?”

Jace didn’t answer immediately. He pulled a data slate from his belt. Swiped his thumb across it. The slate chirped.

He held it out to Linnea. The screen glowed blue.

“Look,” he said.

She didn’t take it. “Why?”

“Because I found something else in the data,” Jace said. “You funded this place. For years.”

The words landed like a physical blow. Linnea’s breath stopped.

The enforcers shifted. Rourke’s eyes flicked to the slate.

Linnea stared at Jace. “What?”

He tapped the slate. The display changed. Ledgers. Millions of credits. Transfers from an anonymous account to Corio Station’s operational budget.

“You,” she whispered.

Jace nodded once. “Every safety override. Every undocumented repair. Every credit that kept this station from collapsing—it came from you.”

Rourke made a sound. A laugh, half swallowed. “You were bleeding yourself dry to keep this rust bucket in orbit?”

Linnea didn’t look at him. Her eyes stayed on Jace.

“Why?” she asked.

He didn’t look away. “Because I read the logs. The ones you hid in the bypass. The ones that matched the failures.”

“And?”

“And I realized you were the only one keeping us alive.” Jace tapped the slate again. “I traced the credits. The anonymous donor? It was you.”

Linnea’s pulse hammered in her throat. She wanted to deny it. Wanted to say it wasn’t true. But the numbers on the slate didn’t lie.

Rourke’s comm buzzed. He thumbed it open. Listened. Then killed the comm.

“Enough,” he said. “Take her to the holding cell. I’ll deal with her when Earth’s team arrives.”

Jace didn’t move. “Sir, I have to escort her.”

Rourke’s eyes flicked to Linnea. “You’re compromised.”

“Not yet.” Jace’s voice was firm. “I’m still under OBA orders.”

Linnea watched the two men. The tension between them was a live wire.

Rourke stepped forward. “She’s a fugitive. A saboteur. A thief.”

Jace didn’t flinch. “She’s the reason this station hasn’t torn itself apart.”

Rourke’s hand twitched. “You’re choosing her over your oath?”

Jace’s gaze didn’t waver. “I’m choosing the truth.”

Linnea swallowed. The cuffs dug into her wrists. The magnets were still active. Still locking her in place.

But for the first time in years, she didn’t feel trapped.

Rourke’s comm buzzed again. He thumbed it open. Listened. Then killed the comm.

He turned to the enforcers. “Take her down. Now.”

The enforcers moved.

Jace stepped between them and Linnea. “Not her.”

Rourke’s face darkened. “Captain, stand down.”

Jace didn’t move. “Sir, this is my jurisdiction. I’m taking her into protective custody.”

Linnea’s breath hitched. The word hung between them like a promise.

Protective.

Rourke’s eyes flicked to Linnea. Then back to Jace.

The transfer deck was silent. The air scrubbers wheezed. The station creaked around them.

Rourke exhaled through his nose. “Fine. Take her. But you’ll answer for this.”

Jace didn’t look back as he guided Linnea toward the service corridor. His hand was firm on her arm, but not cruel.

She walked beside him, cuffed but not broken.

The enforcers didn’t follow.

Rourke watched them go. His comm buzzed again. He thumbed it open. Listened. Then killed the comm.

Linnea’s mind raced. The credits. The lies. The truth.

Jace led her through the corridor. The lights flickered. The station groaned.

She finally spoke.

“Why didn’t you tell me?”

Jace kept walking. “Because I didn’t know until today.”

“And now?”

He didn’t answer. Not right away.

Then—

“Now I need to understand.”

She looked at him. Really looked at him.

His face was hard. Determined. But there was something else in his eyes.

Something like regret.

Something like hope.

The station creaked again. The lights flickered once more.

Linnea didn’t pull away from Jace’s grip.

She didn’t resist.

For the first time in years, she let someone else lead.

Chapter 14

The OBA interim director’s office smelled of ozone and old coffee. The walls were lined with reinforced durasteel, the floor a single seamless slab of carbon-fiber composite. Linnea sat across from him, fingers curled around the edge of the table. Her boots didn’t quite touch the ground.

Director Harkin was a gaunt man with a voice like gravel. He tapped his pen against a data slate. “You want full pardon. Full restoration of patents. A seat on the design review board.” He exhaled through his nose. “And Rourke gets civilian exile.”

Linnea didn’t blink. “Yes.”

Harkin leaned back in his chair. The springs groaned. “That’s a steep price for silence.”

“It’s the only way your oversight committees won’t dig into how this station was actually built.”

Jace stood by the door, arms crossed. His uniform jacket was unbuttoned at the collar. His eyes stayed on Linnea, steady and unreadable.

Harkin’s pen stilled. “You’re asking for a lot. Especially after the sabotage allegations.”

Linnea tilted her head. “You mean the sabotage that kept this station from collapsing during the last pressure cycle? The one that would’ve killed everyone on board if Rourke’s ‘flawless’ designs had held?”

Harkin didn’t answer.

Jace stepped forward. “Sir, we have the logs. The structural overrides. The hidden repairs. All attributable to Director Rourke’s direct orders.”

Harkin’s gaze flicked between them. “And the patents?”

Linnea slid a data chip across the table. It clinked against the slate. “Original schematics. Encrypted. Timestamped. Every modification I made to keep this place from tearing itself apart.”

Harkin didn’t touch it. “You could’ve come forward earlier.”

“Would you have believed me?” Linnea asked. “Or would you have labeled me a traitor and handed me to Rourke’s firing squad?”

The office was silent. The hum of the station’s life support was a distant whisper.

Harkin sighed. Then he picked up the data chip. Slotted it into his slate. Waited. His eyebrows lifted.

“This is comprehensive.”

Linnea nodded. “I designed Corio Station. From the alloy skins to the non-standard energy grid. Every critical node. Every fail-safe. Every secret I buried to keep it standing.”

Harkin removed the chip. Handed it back. “And Rourke’s exile?”

Linnea stood. The chair legs scraped. “He gets to live in a civilian habitat. No title. No authority. Just a quiet corner of the Jovian system where he can’t hurt anyone else.”

Harkin rubbed his temples. “You’re not asking for amnesty. You’re asking for justice.”

“Justice is just a word,” Linnea said. “What I want is for the people who actually built this place to be credited. For the ones who were erased to have their names back.”

Harkin exhaled. Nodded. “I’ll draft the terms. You’ll sign in the morning.”

Linnea turned toward the door.

Jace stepped aside. Let her pass.

She walked into the corridor. The air was cooler here. The lights were bright. She could feel Jace behind her, close but not touching.

He spoke before she reached the lift.

“What about the love interest?”

Linnea stopped. Didn’t turn around. But her fingers twitched at her sides.

Jace continued, voice low. “You mentioned someone once. Back in The Edge. Said he was your only friend.”

Linnea turned slowly. Met his eyes. For the first time in years, she smiled.

Not a cold curve of the lips. Not a polite upturn. A real smile.

It felt strange. Like her face had forgotten how.

Jace stared at her. His expression didn’t change. But something in his posture shifted.

The lift doors opened behind her. She stepped inside. Didn’t wait.

Jace followed. The doors sealed.

They stood in silence as the lift descended.

Then Jace spoke again. Quieter this time.

“You smiled.”

Linnea looked at him. The lift hummed. The numbers above the door counted down.

She didn’t answer.

But she didn’t look away either.

The lift stopped. The doors opened.

The memorial was already being prepared in the central spire. Rourke’s name would be etched today. Then crossed out. Replaced with hers.

Jace walked beside her. His shoulder nearly brushed hers.

She didn’t move away.

Not this time.

Chapter 15

The central spire’s memorial hall was a cathedral of steel and light. The air smelled of new welds and polished durasteel. Names glowed along the curved walls—too many to count, too many to read. The names of the dead. The builders. The forgotten.

Rourke’s name was there. Freshly etched. A line already drawn through it.

Linnea stood at the edge of the crowd, fingers curled around the railing. The spire’s gravity kept her grounded, but the height made her pulse hum anyway. Below, the crew moved like a single organism. They laughed. They clinked glasses. They raised voices in toasts to the men and women whose backs had bent for the colony.

Jace materialized beside her without sound. His security jacket had been swapped for a service uniform, the sleeves rolled to the elbows. A smudge of grease darkened his collar.

“You’re not drinking,” he said.

Linnea didn’t look at him. “I’m working.”

He followed her gaze to the names. To the line through Rourke’s.

“Funny how the right names get remembered,” he said.

She exhaled through her nose. “Funny how some people think they earn their legacy by erasing everyone else’s.”

Jace leaned on the railing. His knuckles were raw from last night’s repair work. “You could’ve let this moment belong to you. You’re the one who made it real.”

Linnea’s fingers tightened. “I didn’t do it for the recognition.”

“Then why?”

She turned then. Met his eyes. The memorial lights cast half his face in shadow. The other half showed exhaustion. And something else. Something she didn’t want to name.

“Because someone had to.”

Jace held her gaze. The noise of the crowd faded. The spire hummed around them, a quiet vibration in the bones of the station.

Then he smiled. Just a flicker. Just for her.

Linnea looked away first.

The crew began to sing. A slow, rough chorus from the early days of the colony. The words were old. The tune was older. Linnea didn’t know the song, but she knew the weight of it.

Jace dropped his voice to match the music. “You’re not joining them.”

She shook her head. “Not my ceremony.”

He didn’t press. Just stood there. Close enough to brush against her sleeve if either of them moved.

The song ended. Applause rippled through the hall. A crew chief raised a glass high. “To the builders.”

Linnea didn’t lift hers.

Jace nudged her with his shoulder. Not a push. Not an order. Just a nudge.

She exhaled. Lifted her glass. Swallowed half the drink without tasting it.

The crowd thinned. The lights dimmed. The memorial would be sealed until the next anniversary, when fresh names could be added.

Linnea turned away from the railing. “Come on.”

Jace fell in beside her. It felt natural. Like they’d done this a hundred times before.

They didn’t speak as they rode the lift down. The central spire was all polished surfaces and quiet efficiency. The lower levels smelled like metal and recycled air. The Deep Cut’s distinct hum vibrated through the deck plates.

Linnea paused at the maintenance corridor. The same one from seven years ago. The same one where she’d last touched the cracked data slate.

The slate was gone.

Welded shut.

A fresh scar in the wall.

Jace stopped beside her. “That’s new.”

Linnea reached out. Traced the weld with her fingertip. The metal was still warm.

“They couldn’t scrub it,” she said. “So they buried it.”

Jace crouched. Ran his thumb along the seam. “You know what’s inside?”

She didn’t answer.

He stood. Turned the slate in his hands. Examined the weld. The quality of the work. His jaw tightened.

“You do.”

Linnea didn’t confirm. Didn’t deny.

Jace set the slate back on the shelf. It clunked against the bracing.

“What do we do with this?” he asked.

Linnea studied the weld. The careful, destructive precision of it.

“Let it rust,” she said.

Jace picked it up again. Turned it in his hands. Weighted it.

Then he dropped it.

The slate hit the deck with a clang. The weld held. The metal didn’t buckle.

Jace crouched. Picked up a fallen rivet from the deck. A spare. A tool. Nothing more.

He pressed the rivet into the seam. Twisted. The rivet bit into the weld. The slate locked in place.

Linnea watched. Didn’t stop him.

When he stood, his hands were smeared with metal dust.

“You’re not letting it rust,” he said.

She met his eyes. Held them.

“No,” she said. “I’m not.”

Jace didn’t smile. Didn’t gloat. Just turned the slate over in his hands one last time.

Then he walked away.

Linnea stayed where she was. The corridor was quiet. The station hummed around her, alive and breathing.

She listened to Jace’s footsteps fade.

Then she followed.
